10.1.label: Question 10.1
10.1.question:
List the advantages and disadvantages of:
- a market-focused approach
- means-tested State health benefits
- forward funding.

Market-focused approach: +         It is cheaper for the State due to saved care costs and simpler administration.
+         It encourages competition, in both healthcare insurance and healthcare provision.
+         It encourages the population to take better care of itself (as people know they will have to
+         There are no cross‐subsidies between rich and poor, healthy and unhealthy. This will
make it popular with people who see themselves as healthy.
+         There can be lower taxes – or saved costs can be spent elsewhere.
–         Lower security of benefits and greater uncertainty for the public.
–         Not popular with unhealthy and/or poor people and generally against the public spirit.
Means-tested State healthcare benefits: ‐tested State healthcare benefits
+         It may be a cost‐effective way for the State to target benefits to those most in need.
The State may provide little or no benefit to individuals with sufficient private means, and
instead direct resources to the less well‐off to ensure that everyone achieves a certain
minimum standard of living when in ill health.
+         It may be redistributive if taxes are raised from those with more wealth / income, and
means‐tested benefits are then paid to those with little wealth / income.
+         Those who fail the means‐test may be encouraged to return to work.
–         People may be discouraged from providing for themselves.
–         It may create a poverty trap whereby increases in a person’s income (or assets) merely
reduces the value of the State benefits.
–         It may encourage people to squander (or hide) existing wealth so that they have more
chance of satisfying the means test.
–         Those who receive benefits may be discouraged from returning to work.
–         It may be perceived as unfair to those who do provide for themselves.
–         Means‐tested benefits are not taken up by all who are entitled to/or need them.
–         Receipt of benefit might identify an individual as ‘poor’, and hence may discourage
him/her from claiming the benefit.
–         It is perceived by some as ‘degrading’, ie State benefits should be a universal right rather
than a payment to the poor.
Forward funding: +         The fund is not as sensitive to demographic changes compared to PAYG.
+         The system fosters forward planning, so that the government should be able to take the
necessary action (eg changing contribution rates) in enough time to ensure the cost of
paying the benefits remains well covered.
+         The public will have more confidence that the State will be able to provide healthcare
benefits to them when their need arises. This makes the public more willing to contribute
to the scheme and increases the popularity of the government.
–         The money could instead be used, with immediate effect, elsewhere.
–         It is more complex to administer.
–         Assets need to be held and managed. An investment policy will be required, investment
decisions will need to be made and assets will need to be bought and sold.

10.2.label: Question 10.2
10.2.question: Explain how the circumstances of each of the following categories of person might be expected to affect the welfare benefits they are offered by the State: employed, unemployed, student, pensioner, widow(er), war veteran.

Solution: Employed
The employed will be paying through taxation and perhaps national insurance, so should receive
full benefits.
As poverty should not be a problem for most, some sort of cost sharing arrangement could be put
in place (eg paying for drug prescriptions).
These could be waived for certain conditions (eg the poorly paid).
Unemployed
The aim of the State would be to pay for medical costs but, if possible, encourage a ‘return‐to‐
work’ ethos, since the unemployed will not be paying for the benefits through salary taxation.
One argument is therefore to limit the benefits, in either amount or term.
However, if the view is that the unemployment is due to the general state of the economy and
not an individual’s problem, or that the unemployment is actually doing some ‘good’ (eg in the
case of informal carers, or voluntary aid workers) then the benefits may be generous.
Student
The aim of the State would be to pay for all reasonable costs.
The student will eventually be working and should not be penalised for the current temporary
hardship.
It may, however, be fair to limit welfare benefits if the student is already receiving loans or other
financial support from the government.
The extent of financial support from parents may also affect a student’s rights to benefits.
Pensioner
There is no option of work, and generally society should take good care of the elderly.
Benefits should not be restricted, but there may be a preference for regular income as opposed to
lump sum, to reflect the lower expectation of life.
Benefits will, of course, not be related to salary but could instead be related to pension.
Pensioners generally feel that they have been paying all their working life for the ‘cover’, and so
normally expect generous benefits.
This is the case even if the State operates a pay‐as‐you‐go system, where the healthy working
members are really paying for the benefits of the sick elderly.
Some benefits will be given in kind (eg bus passes) or as indemnity benefits.
Widow(er)
As for pensioners, but generally an even lower expectation of life and perhaps a greater need for
more generous benefits to reflect the lack of home care available (ie from a partner).
War veteran
War veterans may be unemployed and suffering from long‐term disability, so medical needs can
be high.
Given that the disability was suffered while serving for the country, benefits should be generous.
Other comments as for pensioners also apply here.

10.3.label: Question 10.3
10.3.question: Explain how the pay as you go (PAYG) system of funding State healthcare and welfare operates and depends on the trust between successive generations in the community.

Solution: The current generation of working age pays for the cost of healthcare and welfare benefits for:
         their children                                                                              [¼]
         their parents and grandparents                                                              [¼]
         themselves.                                                                                 [¼]
However, the major costs arise from benefits for children and senior citizens.                       [½]
Costs for children arise mainly from welfare benefits (eg child allowances paid to parents) and
primary healthcare. Costs for senior citizens arise mainly from welfare benefits (eg old‐age
pensions) and from all forms of healthcare.
The payment is made through:
         earmarked taxation                                                                         [½]
         general taxation revenues (eg income tax, sales tax), when there is a shortfall from
earmarked revenues.                                                                        [½]
So contributions from each individual of working age will rise when the current generation is small
compared to the preceding and the next generation.                                              [½]
The smoothing provided by general tax revenues does not alter the fact that the current
generation pays all the costs, ...                                                                      [½]
... except insofar as the government runs a budget deficit and effectively funds the shortfall by
borrowing.                                                                                        [½]
Each generation effectively promises to pay the costs incurred by their grandparents, parents and
children.                                                                                      [½]
The current generation trusts that the succeeding generation will not abuse the system, ...             [½]
... eg by cutting benefits, rather than increasing contributions or borrowing more.                     [½]
If this trust is well founded, then future support for the current generation will match the support
that they provided for other generations.                                                        [½]
[Maximum 4]

10.4.label: Question 10.4
10.4.question: Outline the objectives that the government may have in this regard, the rationale behind each one and any limitations there might be on its ability to meet these objectives.
10.4.scenario: The government of a developed country is considering the ways in which it can provide health and care benefits (ie direct medical services) and welfare benefits (ie financial benefits when unable to work due to illness).

Solution: The overall objectives of the government might be:
To protect the health of the nation                                                                     [½]
A healthy population will be happier and thus re‐elect the party in power.                              [½]
A healthy workforce will also be more productive and hence increase the gross domestic product
of the country.                                                                             [½]
Encouraging people to keep healthy (eg through education) can help reduce the costs of
healthcare and hence also help to balance the budget.                                                   [½]
To subsidise the poor                                                                                   [½]
The aim is to ensure that even the poorest have access to a basic level of healthcare, such as
access to doctors, medicine and hospitalisation, where necessary, ...                                   [½]
... and thus ensure that all of the population remain healthy.                                          [½]
It may be a key aspect of a government’s attempts to redistribute wealth.                               [½]
To balance the budget                                                                                   [½]
The cost of State‐provided healthcare will be met by contributions.                                     [½]
These may be specific health charges (eg National Insurance contributions in the UK), via general
taxation, or through a combination of the two.                                                 [½]
To follow social culture and/or political promises                                                      [½]
The foundations of healthcare provision, such as providing access to free healthcare for all, tend
to be part of a developed national culture and it would be unpopular to change things radically,
especially in the short term.                                                                    [½]
However, the political ethics of the party in power will have some influence.                           [½]
The extent to which it can meet these objectives will depend on:
         the wealth of the country                                                                     [½]
         the priority given to the provision of healthcare                                             [½]
         the style and culture of politics within the country.                                    [½]
[Maximum 6]

10.5.label: Question 10.5
10.5.scenario: A government is planning to provide State-funded income benefits for those who are unable to earn a living as the result of sickness or injury.

(a).question: A flat benefit equal to 40% of national average earnings. The benefit will be increased each year as the latest data become available. State advantages and disadvantages.

Solution: Flat benefit equal to 40% of national average earnings
+         It is simple to administer, because all claimants get the same benefit.                       [½]
+         The benefit may only be sufficient to cover basic living costs, ...                           [½]
... so, unless pre‐sickness income was lower than the sickness benefit, it will act to
encourage a return to work as soon as possible.                                               [½]
+         The overall claim costs are likely to be lower under this method.                             [½]
–         The benefit may be sufficient for single people, but may unfairly penalise family members
where the individual has dependants. The dependants will suffer through no fault of their
own.                                                                                   [½]
–         It may give lower paid people more income than they were earning when able, ...               [½]
... which would be a significant disincentive for the person to return to work, and would
increase the cost of providing the benefits.                                             [½]
+         The benefit is linked to average earnings, so the claimant will benefit from the economic
growth of the country, that would not be provided by a link to prices.                  [½]
–         For those with well‐paid jobs (and no employer sick pay), the method does not allow them
to maintain their lifestyle in the short term, so there will be an abrupt decline in the
family’s fortunes.                                                                       [½]

(b).question: A benefit of 70% of the individual's average weekly earnings in the last thirteen weeks of employment for the first 26 weeks; then 40% of national average earnings. State advantages and disadvantages.

Solution: First 26 weeks at 70% of the individual’s average weekly earnings, then flat benefit
equal to 40% of national average earnings
–         It is more complicated to administer as the initial benefit is different for each individual,
so the administrative costs of the system will be greater.                                  [½]
–         It may take some time to gather the information required to determine the correct
benefit that should be paid, resulting in delays to payments.                                 [½]
–         The level of initial benefit only provides a limited incentive to return to work (because it is
quite high).                                                                               [½]
–         Those claimants earning significantly below average earnings will be worse off than under
the flat benefit scheme, ...                                                           [½]
... and as a result they may have insufficient income to live on, unless they are eligible for
other welfare benefits.                                                                     [½]
–         The reference period of 13 weeks may not be long enough to reflect the impact of
bonuses and similar payments on a claimant’s pre‐sickness income.                                  [½]
+         The initial benefit is at such a level that there will not be an abrupt change in lifestyle as a
result of sickness.                                                                           [½]
+         It is less likely that benefits (in the first 26 weeks) will exceed normal earnings, and so an
incentive to return to work remains.                                                         [½]
For those remaining sick for at least 26 weeks, the benefit level will change to be the same
as (a) and so the same advantages and disadvantages described in (a) will apply.          [½]
–         Overall the benefits are likely to have a higher cost.                                       [½]
[Maximum 6]

10.6.label: Question 10.6
10.6.scenario: In Country A all health and care is free at the point of use. In Country B the State reimburses a fixed percentage of care costs (e.g. Major Surgery 95%, Minor Surgery 80%, GP/Specialist Visits 70%, etc.).

(i).question: Describe the advantages and disadvantages to the State of each system.

Advantages and disadvantages: Country A: +         The system ensures that all those who need medical treatment receive it according to
their clinical need, and not their ability to pay.                                   [½]
–         It may encourage inappropriate use of health and care as it is free to the user, ...               [½]
... eg seeking health and care advice for what are personal and social difficulties rather
than genuine medical problems.                                                             [½]
+         All resources can be devoted to providing treatment, rather than collecting fees from
patients.                                                                             [½]
–         It might cause consumers to under‐value health and care because they have no idea of
the costs involved, ...                                                              [½]
... eg missing appointments, not taking prescribed medicines etc.                                  [½]
–         Controls on the costs of the service may only be possible through third‐party providers.[½]
+         PMI is not essential, and so regulation of insurers (although essential to protect
consumers) will not be a major task.                                                               [½]
+         It should be popular with consumers (if the system works well).                                    [½]
–         It may generate excess demand and result in waiting lists for operations, ...                      [½]
... and perhaps the need to ration expensive treatments.                                           [½]
–         There is no profit motive, and so there will be little effort to control costs and little
incentive for staff to be efficient and give good customer service.                                [½]
–         Because there are no costs involved in ill health, people are not encouraged to look after
themselves.                                                                              [½]
–         It does nothing to encourage commerce / competition for health and care provision.                 [½]
Advantages and disadvantages to the State:
Advantages and disadvantages: Country B: +         Consumers will appreciate the costs of health and care, …                                  [½]
… which should encourage them to look after themselves and only seek treatment when it
is necessary.                                                                     [½]
This exercises (limited) control on the demand for services.                               [½]
+         Those who pay are less likely to abuse the system (eg always keeping appointments, or
cancelling them if they are not needed).                                              [½]
+         Providers are allowed to charge above‐tariff prices, which may make consumers more
aware of the quality of care and the expertise of those providing it.              [½]
This greater appreciation should help the State when it needs to explain health and care
choices to consumers.                                                                  [½]
+         The published price tariff is a mechanism whereby the State can exercise some control
over the costs of the service.                                                        [½]
+         By varying the percentages of the costs met by the State, the government will be able to
vary the impact of health costs.                                                       [½]
For example, consumers make a greater contribution to primary care (this is illustrated in
the example given in the question).                                                     [½]
–         There will need to be a PMI market (basic policies to pay the patient contribution needed
for tariff prices, and superior cover to pay the higher contribution needed for treatment
by providers charging non‐tariff prices).                                               [½]
–         All consumers will need insurance and so there will need to be comprehensive regulation
of insurers to protect consumers.                                                     [½]
–         If prices for basic PMI cover are too high for the poorest consumers, it may create an
‘underclass’ who do not receive adequate treatment.                                        [½]
This consequence may be unpopular with many voters (not just the poorest who are
affected in this way).                                                                     [½]
+         Insurers will have an interest in the costs of all care because basic PMI will pay a
proportion of the costs of every treatment. So the insurers will be another force acting to
control prices.                                                                         [½]
+         Competition will encourage innovation in treatments and the ways in which they are
provided.                                                                          [½]
[Maximum 10]

(ii).question: Describe how each system might influence the market for private medical insurance in that country.

Country A: The market will be confined to those with higher incomes (and better health), ...                        [½]
... and because cover is optional, premium levels are unlikely to be regulated.                          [½]
Insurers will need to design products that offer benefits that are superior to the free service that
... eg treatment when and where the consumer wants it, and superior non‐medical care.                    [½]
The market will be influenced by economic prosperity (buoyant in good times and sluggish in
... and by the success of the free service (eg long waiting lists for treatment or lack of treatment
choice will increase the demand for insurance).                                                     [½]
Country B: Some PMI cover is essential, and so there will be a large market for basic policies.                     [½]
This will help to pool risks and spread overhead expenses, but will mean that policy servicing costs
will be a large proportion of the premium.                                                       [½]
The large market will mean more competition between insurers, and may lead to a more efficient
As basic cover will be bought by nearly everybody, some price control will be likely.                    [½]
This may mean that community rating is imposed, which will make it harder for insurers to charge
There will be a market for policies that meet the costs of care at non‐tariff rates. This market will
be confined to those with higher incomes.                                                         [½]
Insurers will need to design products that provide extra benefits that consumers want to buy.
This might be easier than in Country A, because all consumers already have some experience of

10.7.label: Question 10.7
10.7.scenario: A State previously provided free medical care to all citizens via registered doctors. The government proposes to charge patients a fee per consultation (~5% of average weekly wage), refundable for welfare-benefit recipients through means-testing.

(i).question: Outline the advantages and disadvantages of this proposal.

Solution: for doctors and the State
+         The proposal is likely to reduce demands on doctors’ time, ...                                 [½]
... for example by encouraging self‐diagnosis / medication of minor illnesses.                 [½]
+         The existence of the refund means that the charging system is progressive, ...               [½]
... ie those with higher incomes pay more.                                                   [½]
+         It will give a higher profile to healthcare and may encourage individuals to look after
themselves.                                                                                  [½]
+         The additional income will act to reduce the financial burden on the State healthcare
system.                                                                                      [½]
–         There are no provisions for unemployed persons who are not receiving welfare benefits,
such as students and children.                                                       [½]
–         Because the fee is paid on consultation, it may encourage patients who have made
appointments not to keep them.                                                               [½]
–         It may increase costs of care in the long term because patients may delay seeking advice
and treatment if they have to pay for consultations, ...                               [½]
... for example, being deterred from seeking advice about angina may result in more
expensive treatment for cardiac problems.                                                    [½]
–         The change may not be politically acceptable in a system where doctors’ consultations
have previously been free.                                                            [½]
–         It may encourage doctors to recommend (free) out‐patient care for the chronically ill,
when doctor‐managed care may be cheaper and more effective, so merely transfers costs
in the system.                                                                         [½]
–/+       It may require a potentially large and expensive administrative system.                      [½]
However, only a small proportion of the population will be entitled to refunds and the use
of electronic systems should minimise costs.                                            [½]
for patients
+         It should improve access to doctors in that patients should not have to wait as long to see
a doctor.                                                                                [½]
+         The additional income the State system receives might be used to improve the quality of
health and care, reduce taxes or the level of contributions the public make to the State
scheme or be used for other purpose, such as education.                                  [½]
–         It may cause ‘cashflow’ problems for the less well off: they may find it difficult to make
cash payments out of their limited income.                                                 [½]
–         Unless suitable provisions are made, the system may reduce care or increase the costs of
care for the chronically ill, ...                                                     [½]
... eg those needing regular blood pressure checks may be seen less often.              [½]
[Maximum 7]

(ii).question: Assuming the proposal is implemented, describe the opportunities for the marketing of new products that it may present for a major provider of health and care insurance.

Solution: The less well‐off will have little need for insurance because many are likely to be entitled to
refunds. The very well off will see no need for insurance if the amounts involved can be met from
their regular income.                                                                           [½]
However, those on middle incomes may see a need for insurance to cover the cash payments they
expect to make under the new system.                                                      [½]
The insurer is already a major provider of health and care insurance. This means its current
products are well accepted by those ‘opting out’ of the State system in favour of private
secondary and tertiary care.                                                                             [½]
Even though these policyholders are unlikely to have an immediate need for insurance to cover
the cash payment for primary care (because they are likely to be well off), the insurer could
include the benefit in current policies.                                                      [½]
The additional administration expenses associated with the payment of the benefits are likely to
be significant.                                                                                [½]
These expenses could be minimised by making the benefit subject to an excess, ...                        [½]
... or perhaps by requiring that all payments are accumulated and a single claim is made at the
end of the policy year.                                                                         [½]
The insurer might be able to use its brand or reputation to market a cash plan to individuals who
do not currently have PMI. This would provide a partial or full refund of the consultation fee. [½]
A significant risk will be in determining the claim incidence rates under an entirely new system.[½]
However, the claim size is fixed by the formula that has been adopted.                                   [½]
Offering more comprehensive benefits than are necessary to address the changes in the State
system, such as physiotherapy or dental care, will increase the premium but may well increase the
appeal of the policy.                                                                         [½]
This may also provide a way of spreading the fixed costs.                                                [½]
Any method that would reduce the selling costs of the policy would be advantageous,
eg marketing group schemes through employers / affinity groups.                                          [½]
Group schemes through employers could be enhanced by offering benefits that reduce
